1: Koh amar YHWH lech veqanita baqbuq yotser cheres — thus says YHWH, Go and buy a potter's clay flask (baqbûq). The baqbûq is a narrow-necked bottle that makes a gurgling sound (baqbaq) when poured — onomatopoeic naming from the liquid-sound. Unlike the wet clay of ch18 (which could be reshaped), the fired ceramic flask is fixed; once broken, it cannot be repaired. This distinction between the two oracle-vessels is the structural key to the sequence. Umiziqney haˁam — some of the elders of the people: witnesses are required for the prophetic sign-act, giving it legal standing.
2: Veyatsata el-gey ven-Hinnom asher petach shaˁar hacharsit — go out to the valley of Ben-hinnom at the entrance of the Potsherd Gate. The Potsherd Gate (šaʿar heharsît) is the gate through which pottery shards (broken vessels) were discarded — the potter's dump. The location makes the symbolic action visible: bringing a whole flask to the place where broken pottery accumulates, then smashing it.
3: Vequrata sham et-divrey asher adaber eilecha — and proclaim there the words I speak to you. The double audience — kings of Judah (malkey Yehuda) and people of Jerusalem — frames the oracle as a formal legal proclamation before witnesses. Hineni mevi raˁa el-hamakom hazze — I am bringing disaster on this place. Tsalal oznav (his ears will ring) — the resonance of the news will be physically experienced.
4: Three charges: ˁazavuni (they abandoned me); wayenakru et-hamakom hazze (made this place foreign — nāḵrî, alien to its covenant identity); and offered incense to gods asher lo yadaˁum (that they did not know — the yādaʿ of covenant relationship: gods with whom there is no covenant bond). Umileu et-hamakom hazze dam neqiim — they have filled this place with the blood of the innocent: the child sacrifice that will be specified in v5.
5: Uvanyu et-bamat haBaˁal lisrof et-benehem baesh ˁolot laBaˁal — they have built the high places of Baal to burn their sons as burnt offerings to Baal. The horror of child sacrifice (Molek-tophet cult) is given its most explicit description. The threefold denial that YHWH ever sanctioned this: asher lo-tsiviti velo-dibbarti velo-ˁalta ˁal-libbi — which I never commanded, never spoke of, never entered my mind. The escalating negations move from formal command (ṣāwâ) to spoken word (dibbēr) to even mental conception (ˁālâ ʿal-lēḇ) — the total repudiation goes beyond law to reject the cultic innovation at the deepest level of divine consciousness.
6: Lachen hinneh yamim baim neˁum YHWH velo yiqare lamakom hazze ˁod haTofet ugey ven-Hinnom ki im-gey haharega — therefore the days are coming when this place will no longer be called Tophet or the Valley of Ben-hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter (gêʾ hahereḡâ). The renaming is a prophetic speech act — the new name embodies the new reality. Tophet (possibly from tōp, drum, used to drown out the cries of child sacrifice, or from an Aramaic word for fireplace) is renamed to announce its fate as a mass grave.
7: Vebaqoti et-ˁatsat Yehuda viYerushalayim bamakom hazze — I will empty out (ûḇaqqōtî, from bāqaq) the plans of Judah and Jerusalem in this place. The verb bāqaq (to empty, to pour out) is a deliberate pun on baqbûq (the flask, v1) — YHWH will do to Judah's plans what will be done to the flask: pour them out, empty them. The wordplay grounds the sign-act interpretation linguistically. Vehifaltem beherev lifney oyvehem — I will make them fall by the sword before their enemies.
8: Venatattiha leshshama ulishriqah — I will make this city a desolation and a thing to be hissed at (šerîqâ, the onomatopoeic hissing sound of v16:16 — the passerby's audible shock). Kol ˁover ˁaleyha yisham vishraq ˁal-kol-makkoteyha — everyone who passes will be appalled and hiss at all its wounds (makkôt, blows/plagues). The wound-metaphor for a city's destruction is vivid: the city has been struck, and the marks remain visible to passersby.
9: Vehoˁalti otam basar benehem uvesar benoteihem — I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and daughters. The extreme of siege cannibalism is the most severe covenant curse (cf. Deut 28:53-57; Lev 26:29) — when the siege cuts off food supply, the ultimate boundary is crossed. The phrase uvmatsor uvamatsok asher yetsiru lahem oyvehem — in the desperate siege and distress that their enemies impose on them — grounds the prediction in the historical reality of siege warfare rather than making it supernatural intervention.
10: Ushavarta et-habbaqbuq leˁeney haanashim haholchim itach — then break the flask before the men who have come with you. The physical act of smashing the fired clay vessel completes the sign-act. The witnesses see the irreversibility: fired clay shattered into fragments cannot be put back together. This is the decisive difference from ch18 — malleable clay can be reshaped, but the broken flask is an image of judgment completed beyond repair.
11: Koh amar YHWH tsvaot kachah eshbor et-haˁam hazze veˁet haˁir hazze — thus says YHWH of hosts: so I will break this people and this city as one breaks a potter's vessel that can never be repaired (asher lo-yukhal lehirafeˁ ˁôd). The word rāpāʾ (repair/heal) is the same verb used of healing wounds and diseases throughout the OT prophets — including Jeremiah's own promise of healing in 30:17. Here it is negated: the break is beyond healing by ordinary means.
12: Ken eˁaseh lemakom hazze neˁum YHWH — so I will do to this place. The oracle is grounded in the sign-act: the smashing of the flask enacts the divine decision, making it present and certain before it is historically accomplished. The sign-acts of Jeremiah are not merely illustrative but performative — they participate in the reality they depict.
13: Vehayeta batey Yerushalayim uvitey malkey Yehuda ketomeh kemakom haTofet — the houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah will be defiled like Tophet. Lechol habatim asher qitteru ˁal-gaggotehem lechol tsva hashamayim — all the houses on whose rooftops they burned incense to all the host of heaven. Rooftop incense-burning (gag, roof) is the specific cultic practice associated with astral worship — the host of heaven (ṣĕḇāʾ haššāmayim) refers to the sun, moon, and stars as divine beings, a Mesopotamian influence that Josiah's reform had attempted to eradicate (2 Kings 23:5).
14: Wayavo Yirmeyahu min-haTofet asher shlaho YHWH sham lehinnave — Jeremiah returned from Tophet where YHWH had sent him to prophesy. The oracle at Tophet is complete; Jeremiah now moves to the temple court (chatsar beit YHWH) for the second public proclamation to a wider audience.
15: Hineni mevi el-haˁir hazze veˁal kol-ˁarehah et kol-haraˁa asher dibbarti ˁaleyha — I am going to bring on this city and on all its surrounding towns every disaster I have spoken against it. Ki-hiqqashu et-ˁorfam leilti shmoˁa et-devaray — because they have stiffened their neck (hiqqāšû ʾet-ʿorpām, made their neck hard/stiff) so as not to hear my words. The stiff-neck metaphor (qāšeh ʿōrep) is the Exodus tradition's description of Israel's resistance (Exod 32:9; 33:3,5; Deut 9:6,13) — Jeremiah invokes the foundational pattern of covenant resistance that culminated at Sinai.
